metadata.title: niwo | Cybersecurity
metadata.description: Nikita is a cybersecurity student and freelance ethical hacker specializing in web application security, penetration testing, and digital defense.

Home.heroEyebrow: Offensive security for modern applications and agentic systems
Home.heroHeadline: Vulnerabilities don’t wait for your next sprint. Neither should your security.
Home.heroParagraph: I help startups, developers, and small businesses identify vulnerabilities, secure AI-assisted workflows, and strengthen modern applications before attackers do.
Home.supportLine: Web Application Security · DevSecOps · Agentic Security · Penetration Testing
Home.servicesTitle: Security support for modern web application risk
Home.servicesDescription: Focused engagements built to uncover meaningful weaknesses, explain risk clearly, and help teams fix the issues that matter first.

approach.title: A calm, practical approach to security work
approach.description: The goal is straightforward: keep scope focused, test realistically, communicate clearly, and leave teams with findings they can actually use.

scope.title: Focused scope
scope.text: Engagements stay centered on the flows, trust boundaries, and actions that carry real application risk.

testing.title: Realistic testing
testing.text: Testing is grounded in how modern web applications are actually attacked, not just checklist coverage.

reporting.title: Clear reporting
reporting.text: Findings are written to be understandable, defensible, and useful to both technical and non-technical stakeholders.

remediation.title: Practical remediation
remediation.text: Each engagement is meant to help teams fix issues with confidence, not just collect another report.

webAppSecurity.title: Web App Security
webAppSecurity.text: I look at your web app the way an attacker would — then tell you what actually needs fixing and why.

penetrationTesting.title: Penetration Testing
penetrationTesting.text: I try to break in the same way a real attacker would — then show you exactly what worked and what to do about it.

secureDevelopment.title: Secure Development
secureDevelopment.text: I work alongside your team during development so security gets handled early — not rushed in before release.

metadata.title: Web App Security | niwo
metadata.description: Hands-on web application security reviews that surface real vulnerabilities, explain what matters, and give your team a clear path to fix things.

card.title: Web App Security
card.text: I look at your web app the way an attacker would — then tell you what actually needs fixing and why.

hero.title: Find the gaps before someone else does
hero.intro: I dig into your web application with an attacker's perspective — looking at auth flows, access control, input handling, and the configuration details that tend to get overlooked. The goal is to surface the issues that actually matter, not just run a scanner and hand you a PDF.
hero.outcomeText: A clear picture of where your app is exposed, which issues are worth prioritizing, and specific guidance on what to fix first — without the noise.

covers.title: What this covers
items: Your app's attack surface — user flows, exposed endpoints, and anything public-facing
Auth and session handling: login, password reset, token management, and the assumptions behind them
Access control gaps — privilege escalation, broken object-level access, missing checks
Input handling: injection vectors, unsafe parsing, weak validation
Config and deployment issues that quietly widen the attack surface

steps.title: Get the lay of the land
steps.text: I start by understanding how the app is supposed to work — where sensitive actions happen, what the trust boundaries are, and where things could go wrong.

steps.title: Go after the weak spots
steps.text: I test the routes, states, and edge cases that tend to hide real issues — not just the obvious stuff a checklist would catch.

steps.title: Write it up so you can act on it
steps.text: Every finding comes with context, impact, and concrete next steps. No vague risk ratings — just what's broken and how to fix it.

bestFor.title: Best for
items: Startups getting ready to ship or scale something customer-facing
Small teams that want a real security look without a heavyweight process
Products dealing with user accounts, permissions, or sensitive data
Developers who'd rather get actionable findings than a 60-page report they'll never read

deliverables.title: What you get
items: A short, focused report with validated findings and severity context
Remediation guidance for each issue — specific enough to hand to a developer
A prioritized list so you know what to tackle first
Follow-up support if anything in the report needs clarification

cta.title: Want to know where your app is exposed?
cta.text: Whether you're preparing for launch, recovering from a change, or just want a second pair of eyes — I can take a look.

metadata.title: Penetration Testing | niwo
metadata.description: Realistic penetration testing for web applications — validated attack paths, clear impact, and findings you can actually act on.

card.title: Penetration Testing
card.text: I try to break in the same way a real attacker would — then show you exactly what worked and what to do about it.

hero.title: See what an attacker would actually get away with
hero.intro: This isn't checkbox security. I test your application the way someone trying to break it would — chaining weaknesses, escalating access, and seeing how far things go. The point is to show you what's actually exploitable, not just what's theoretically wrong.
hero.outcomeText: Validated findings with real impact context, a clear sense of what to fix first, and enough detail to hand things off to your developers without back-and-forth.

covers.title: What this covers
items: Hands-on testing of exposed features, high-risk workflows, and critical user actions
Actual exploitation — not just flagging potential issues, but proving what's reachable
Attack chain exploration where smaller bugs combine into bigger problems
Findings interpreted by risk and impact, not just scanner severity scores
Reporting that's written for humans, not compliance checkboxes

steps.title: Scope it around what matters
steps.text: We figure out together which systems, flows, and assumptions carry the most risk — so I spend time where it counts, not everywhere at once.

steps.title: Attack realistically, document thoroughly
steps.text: I go after paths that are actually exploitable and document everything clearly enough that the results hold up under scrutiny.

steps.title: Deliver findings you can use immediately
steps.text: The report is built to support fixing things — reproduction steps, impact context, and prioritized recommendations without the filler.

bestFor.title: Best for
items: Teams about to launch, scale, or face a customer security review
Products that need someone external to actually try breaking in
Organizations tired of noisy scan results that don't tell them what matters
Applications with complex access control, sensitive logic, or high-value data

deliverables.title: What you get
items: A findings report with severity, impact, and reproduction steps for each issue
Remediation guidance that's specific enough to turn into tickets
A prioritized breakdown so you know where to start
A walkthrough call if you want to go through the findings together

cta.title: Want to know how your app holds up under real pressure?
cta.text: If you'd rather find out from me than from an attacker, let's set up a test.

metadata.title: Secure Development | niwo
metadata.description: Security support built into your development workflow — catch problems early, ship with more confidence, and stop bolting security on at the end.

card.title: Secure Development
card.text: I work alongside your team during development so security gets handled early — not rushed in before release.

hero.title: Stop fixing security issues that shouldn't have shipped
hero.intro: Most security problems aren't hard to prevent — they just get introduced because nobody was thinking about them at the right time. I plug into your development process early and help catch the patterns that tend to cause issues later: weak auth flows, missing access checks, trust assumptions that don't hold up.
hero.outcomeText: Fewer surprises before release, stronger implementation patterns, and a team that's more confident about the security of what they're shipping.

covers.title: What this covers
items: Reviewing implementation patterns that tend to introduce security problems
Security input during feature development, architecture decisions, and release prep
Early review of auth flows, access control, trust boundaries, and data handling
Helping developers build with safer defaults and fewer regressions
Keeping security in sync with how your team actually builds and ships

steps.title: Get involved early
steps.text: I look at the design, the intended flow, and the trust assumptions while things are still easy to change — not after they're baked in.

steps.title: Give input that fits your workflow
steps.text: I'm not here to add process. I focus on improving the decisions, defaults, and implementation details that matter most — in a way that doesn't slow you down.

steps.title: Help you ship with confidence
steps.text: The end result is code that's more secure by design, not because someone bolted a review onto the end of the sprint.

bestFor.title: Best for
items: Teams building something new or shipping a significant feature change
Developers who want security input they can actually use while coding
Startups that can't afford to get security wrong but also can't afford to slow down
Products where auth, permissions, and sensitive data are central from day one

deliverables.title: What you get
items: Guidance tied to your actual product and how your team works
Notes on risky patterns and where to tighten things up
Recommendations specific enough for developers to apply directly
A stronger security posture that carries forward — not just a one-time review

cta.title: Want security built in from the start?
cta.text: If you'd rather prevent issues than find them after the fact, let's talk about working together during development.

metadata.title: About | niwo
metadata.description: About Nikita, the person behind niwo, working across security, AI, systems, and product design.

About.title: The person behind the system
About.intro: Hey, I'm Nikita. I keep getting drawn to cybersecurity, AI, systems, and product design — mostly because I like figuring out how things actually work under the surface. That usually turns into thinking about where things break, where trust gets assumed instead of earned, and how to build something that doesn't fall apart when it matters.
About.signalText: I care about whether something actually holds up - whether it feels trustworthy, makes sense, and works the way it should when it matters.

drives.title: Mostly just curious about how things work.
drives.text: I like following a system far enough that it starts showing its edges — where it breaks, where it gets fragile, where the assumptions stop being theoretical. That's usually where it gets interesting.

building.title: Figuring out where security, AI, and design overlap.
building.text: That's what I've been spending most of my time on lately. It's the kind of space where you can't get away with surface-level answers — you have to think about whether something actually works in practice, not just in theory. I've also been exploring agent-based workflows with tools like OpenClaw and Codex, trying to understand how software, automation, and reasoning can fit together in ways that are actually useful.

outside.title: I tend to notice the small things.
outside.text: Things like clarity, pacing, and friction — they stand out to me because they're usually what decides whether something feels intuitive or just frustrating. I like clean interfaces, but only when they actually make something easier to use, not just nicer to look at.

metadata.title: Trust Boundary Prototype | niwo
metadata.description: Prototype page for Trust Boundary, a browser-based cybersecurity game concept built inside the NIWO lab.

TrustBoundaryPrototype.title: Trust Boundary is now staged inside the NIWO lab.
TrustBoundaryPrototype.intro: This page is the dedicated route for the Trust Boundary prototype. The game concept stays focused on a small startup office, a compact inbox-driven decision loop, and one short incident path built around trust, ambiguity, and consequences.
TrustBoundaryPrototype.focusTitle: Day 1 is still the target slice.
TrustBoundaryPrototype.focusText: The first playable version should prove one thing clearly: moving through a tiny office, sitting at the desk, reviewing suspicious messages, and making good trust decisions is interesting enough to carry the game.

pillars.office: A small office room gives the prototype a grounded physical frame instead of dropping straight into menus.
pillars.inbox: The inbox remains the center of play, with allow, quarantine, and investigate driving the core decision loop.
pillars.incident: A short incident path should trigger when a bad decision lets a malicious message through.
pillars.summary: The day ends with a readable summary of trust, work continuity, and security judgment.

TrustBoundaryPrototype.terminalBody: Use this command from the homepage terminal to jump straight into the prototype route.
TrustBoundaryPrototype.note: The route is live now so the prototype can be developed in-place inside the NIWO website instead of drifting into a separate app.

objectives.office: Walk to your desk and check the morning inbox.
objectives.officeReady: You are in range. Sit down and start reviewing the day’s messages.
objectives.desk: Review all six messages. Investigate when the call is not obvious.
objectives.incident: A bad decision slipped through. Contain the damage before the day closes.
objectives.summary: Review how your trust calls affected the office.

game.roomPrompt: Use WASD or the arrow keys to move around the office and approach the desk.
game.deskPrompt: Press E or use the desk button to cross into work mode.
game.coworkerHint: Morning. We are waiting on a client reply, so try not to miss anything important.
game.subject: Subject
game.investigationHint: Use Investigate when the message feels plausible but not fully trustworthy.
game.incidentPrompt: A malicious message got trusted and your coworker may have handed over credentials. Choose the best immediate response.
game.summaryStrong: You protected the business without treating every rough edge like a threat.
game.summaryMixed: Some calls were sharp and some were costly. The tension is working.
game.summaryPoor: The office felt the damage. The prototype is showing how quickly trust mistakes compound.
game.briefTitle: A small office. Six messages. One possible incident.
game.briefText: This first slice is meant to prove the whole fantasy in one short loop: arrive, sit down, review ambiguous messages, and live with the operational cost of your calls.

controls.investigate: Investigate before deciding when the message feels plausible

dayPlan.enter: Enter the office and orient yourself in the room.
dayPlan.sit: Sit at the desk and transition into the inbox surface.
dayPlan.review: Resolve six messages with allow, quarantine, or investigate.
dayPlan.incident: Handle one compact incident if a malicious message gets through.
dayPlan.summary: End the day with a readable outcome tied to your decisions.

metadata.title: Imprint | niwo systems
metadata.description: Legal notice for niwo systems. Non-commercial informational website. Professional offers handled privately, outside this platform.

provider.title: 1. Provider Identification
provider.body: Responsible according to Section 5 TMG / EU eCommerce Directive

nature.title: 2. Nature of the Website
nature.body: This website is a non-commercial informational project focused on technology and security research. No goods or services are sold directly through this platform.
nature.disclaimerText: This website does not directly offer paid services. Any professional collaboration or offer resulting from private contact is handled outside this platform, for example by email or a separate contract.

contact.title: 3. Contact
contact.body: For legal notices or formal correspondence, please reach out via legal@niwosystems.com. If a physical mailing address is required for formal service, it will be provided upon request.

liability.title: 4. Liability for Content
liability.body: Content is created with care; however, no warranty is given for completeness or accuracy. Links to external sites are provided for convenience, and responsibility for their content lies with the respective providers.

metadata.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Policy | niwo
metadata.description: How to responsibly report vulnerabilities to the niwo security team.

Vdp.title: Vulnerability Disclosure Policy
Vdp.description: Responsible disclosure guidance for researchers reporting potential security issues.

overview.title: 1. Overview
overview.body: Security is a top priority for us at niwo. We appreciate the work of security researchers and the responsible disclosure of potential vulnerabilities.

scope.title: 2. Scope
scope.body: This policy applies to digital assets owned, operated, or maintained by niwo, including niwosystems.com and associated subdomains.

guidelines.title: 3. Guidelines
items: Do not exploit vulnerabilities beyond what is necessary to demonstrate them.
Do not access or modify user data without consent.
Do not disrupt or degrade our services.
Provide sufficient detail so we can reproduce and validate the issue.

reporting.title: 4. Reporting
reporting.body: If you discover a vulnerability, please report it responsibly by emailing security@niwosystems.com.
reporting.calloutTitle: Include the following information where possible:
items: Description of the vulnerability
Steps to reproduce
Potential impact
Suggested remediation, if available

acknowledgement.title: 5. Acknowledgement
acknowledgement.body: We may publicly acknowledge valid reports that help us improve our security posture. Thank you for helping keep niwo and its users safe.

metadata.title: Privacy Policy | niwo
metadata.description: Privacy policy for this website covering email contact, hosting, and locally hosted fonts.

intro.title: 1. Introduction
intro.body: This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, and protect personal data when you visit this website or contact us by email. We aim to handle information responsibly and transparently in line with applicable data protection law, including the EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR).

controller.title: 2. Data Controller
controller.body: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or your personal data, you can contact us using the details above.

dataWeCollect.title: 3. Data We Collect
dataWeCollect.voluntaryIntro: When you contact us by email, we may process:
voluntaryItems: Your name or signature, if you include it
Your company name, phone number, or other contact details, if you include them
The content of your message and any attachments you send
dataWeCollect.voluntaryOutro: This information is used solely to review and respond to your inquiry.
dataWeCollect.automaticBody: When you visit the website, basic technical data may be processed automatically by hosting infrastructure, such as your IP address, request metadata, browser information, and access logs needed for secure delivery of the site.

purposeLegalBasis.title: 4. Purpose & Legal Basis
purposeLegalBasis.body: We process personal data to operate the website securely, respond to inquiries, and protect our systems against abuse or misuse. Depending on context, the legal basis is either our legitimate interests under Article 6(1)(f) GDPR or steps taken at your request prior to entering into a contract under Article 6(1)(b) GDPR.

storageRetention.title: 5. Data Storage & Retention
storageRetention.body: We retain personal data only for as long as necessary to fulfill the purpose for which it was collected, to communicate with you, or to comply with legal obligations. Email correspondence may be retained where needed to document communication or handle follow-up requests.

thirdParties.title: 6. Use of Third-Party Services
thirdParties.hostingBody: This website is hosted using AWS infrastructure. Hosting providers may process technical connection data and server logs as required to deliver the website securely and reliably.
thirdParties.fontsBody: The website uses locally hosted fonts. No font requests are sent to third-party font providers when loading the site.

sharing.title: 7. Data Sharing
sharing.body: We do not sell personal data. Data is only shared where necessary to operate the website, comply with legal obligations, or protect our rights and systems.

rights.title: 8. Your GDPR Rights
rights.intro: Under the GDPR, you may have the right to:
items: Request access to your personal data
Request correction or deletion of your data
Request restriction of processing
Object to processing in certain circumstances
Lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority
rights.outro: To exercise these rights, contact legal@niwosystems.com.

security.title: 9. Data Security
security.body: We take reasonable technical and organizational measures to protect personal data against unauthorized access, loss, misuse, or disclosure. However, no internet transmission or storage system can be guaranteed to be completely secure.

cookies.title: 10. Cookies & Tracking
cookies.body: This website currently does not rely on non-essential tracking cookies or invasive analytics tooling. If this changes in the future, this policy will be updated accordingly.

changes.title: 11. Changes to This Policy
changes.body: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time to reflect legal, technical, or operational changes. The latest version will always be published on this page.

contact.title: 12. Contact
contact.body: If you have questions about this Privacy Policy or about how your personal data is handled, contact legal@niwosystems.com.
